coredata.dc:title: Probing interactions and dynamics in ether-functionalized ionic liquid electrolytes using 17O NMR spectroscopy and molecular modelling

coredata.dc:description: Ether-functionalized ionic liquids (ILs) are designed to solvate Li/Na and promote their transport. Despite its potential to probe solvation and dynamics, 17O nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy remains underutilized in studying these systems due to experimental challenges. This study explores the interactions and dynamics of IL oxygen groups with Li/Na ions in systems, combining 17O NMR with computational modelling studies using molecular dynamics and density functional theory calculations. We report the first 17O NMR data obtained from IL electrolytes with high concentrations of Li/Na salts, without isotopic enrichment or solvent dilution. Higher temperatures (around 373 K) permit useful spectra to be obtained from these materials at moderate magnetic field strengths where room temperature spectra can show no signals at all due to peak broadening. Focusing on 1-methyl-1-(2-methoxyethyl) pyrrolidinium bis(fluorosulfonyl)imide with LiFSI and NaFSI, the chemical environment of the cation and anion oxygens are revealed to remain unchanged across temperatures. As salt concentration increases, 17O NMR shows a net shielding of the four oxygens of [FSI], with a larger effect in the Li system indicating a stronger interaction with the anion. The cation oxygen shielding was much less sensitive due to the weak cation-Li/Na interaction but did result in an observable deshielding in the high concentration Li system. Modelling results are used to interpret these shifts, while the 17O line widths and T 1 relaxation times provide information on ion dynamics, showing slower dynamics at higher salt content, and in the Na-ion system compared to the Li-ion.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structure and abbreviations of the ether functionalized cations and perfluorinated anions, and TEGDME – G4.
17O NMR spectra at various temperatures for neat [C2O1mpyr][FSI] (a), with 20 mol% LiFSI (b), and with 50 mol% LiFSI (c).

17O NMR spectra of various ether-functionalized ionic liquids obtained at 363 K, while the [C2moxa][FSI] and TEGDME – G4 are obtained at 343 K and 333 K, respectively. All samples are used as neat without metal salts and solvents, except for [C2moxa][FSI] that is with 50 mol% LiFSI. The blue/red broken boxes in the NMR spectra correspond to the blue/red labelled atoms of their respective organic structures. The number of scans for each IL samples are shown on the right side of the NMR spectra. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

17O ∆δ[FSI] (a) and ∆δ[C2O1mpyr] (b) of [C2O1mpyr][FSI] with 20 mol% and 50 mol% LiFSI and NaFSI. The straight lines on the y-axis at zero represent the neat IL. The displacement of δ of neat IL and its salt counterpart is calculated by ∆δ = δneat IL with salt – δneat IL. The ∆δ[FSI] are obtained at various temperatures. The lines are used for guidelines only. While for the ∆δ[C2O1mpyr], the sample temperature was only at 363 K.

Radial distribution function (solid lines) and the corresponding coordination number (dashed lines) of the Li/Na with oxygens of the [FSI] anion (a & b), nitrogen atom of the [FSI] anion with Li/Na (c & d), and oxygen atom of the [C2O1mpyr] cation to Li/Na (e & f) at 20 mol% (left) and 50 mol% (right) salt concentrations. The simulated temperature was 363 K.

SDF of metal ions surrounding the [FSI] anion in ILs with 20 and 50 mol% LiFSI (a and b) and NaFSI (c and d). The colour codes for [FSI] anion are red oxygen, yellow sulfur, blue nitrogen and pink fluorine. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
17O LW[FSI] (a) and 17O LW[C2O1mpyr] (b) of [C2O1mpyr][FSI] with various concentrations of LiFSI and NaFSI at various temperatures. Lines are used for guidelines only.
Anion 17O T 1 relaxation time measurements of [C2O1mpyr][FSI] at various Li/Na salt concentrations. The dashed line indicates the theoretical fitting of the 17O T 1 values for each IL system.

19F T 1 relaxation time measurements of [C2O1mpyr][FSI] with LiFSI and NaFSI at various concentrations, 20 mol% and 50 mol%. The dashed line indicates the theoretical fitting of the 19F T 1 values for each IL system.

17O T 1 relaxation time measurements of [FSI] anion in [C2O1mpyr][FSI] with LiFSI (a) and NaFSI (b) refitted using 19F T 1 parameters corresponding to the dynamics of the fluorine nuclei, Ea and τc. The dashed line indicates the theoretical fitting of the 17O T 1 values for each IL system.

Rotational correlation times (τc) from 19F T 1 and 17O T 1 values of the anion, obtained from T 1 relaxation time measurements in NMR (a) and MD simulations (b). Red is for samples with LiFSI, while blue is with NaFSI. There is no observed τc data from NMR for the IL with 50 mol% NaFSI due to peak broadening at lower temperatures. The τc values of 19F and 17O from MD simulations are obtained from the sulfur‑fluorine bond and sulfur‑oxygen bond in [FSI] anion, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

17O T 1 relaxation time measurements of the [C2O1mpyr] cation in [C2O1mpyr][FSI] at various Li/Na salt concentrations. The dashed line indicates the theoretical fitting from the Arrhenius expression of the 17O T 1 values for each IL system.
Relative molar ratios of various ions present in the IL electrolytes studied.
Fitting parameters from the anion 17O T 1 relaxation time measurements of [C2O1mpyr][FSI] at various Li/Na salt concentrations.
Fitting parameters from 19F T 1 relaxation time measurements of [C2O1mpyr][FSI] at various Li/Na salt concentrations.
Fitting Parameters from the 17O T 1 relaxation time measurements of the [C2O1mpyr] cation in [C2O1mpyr][FSI] at various Li/Na salt concentrations.

head.ce:title: Probing interactions and dynamics in ether-functionalized ionic liquid electrolytes using 17O NMR spectroscopy and molecular modelling

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ether-functionalized ionic liquids (ILs) are designed to solvate Li/Na and promote their transport. Despite its potential to probe solvation and dynamics, 17O nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy remains underutilized in studying these systems due to experimental challenges. This study explores the interactions and dynamics of IL oxygen groups with Li/Na ions in systems, combining 17O NMR with computational modelling studies using molecular dynamics and density functional theory calculations. We report the first 17O NMR data obtained from IL electrolytes with high concentrations of Li/Na salts, without isotopic enrichment or solvent dilution. Higher temperatures (around 373 K) permit useful spectra to be obtained from these materials at moderate magnetic field strengths where room temperature spectra can show no signals at all due to peak broadening. Focusing on 1-methyl-1-(2-methoxyethyl) pyrrolidinium bis(fluorosulfonyl)imide with LiFSI and NaFSI, the chemical environment of the cation and anion oxygens are revealed to remain unchanged across temperatures. As salt concentration increases, 17O NMR shows a net shielding of the four oxygens of [FSI], with a larger effect in the Li system indicating a stronger interaction with the anion. The cation oxygen shielding was much less sensitive due to the weak cation-Li/Na interaction but did result in an observable deshielding in the high concentration Li system. Modelling results are used to interpret these shifts, while the 17O line widths and T 1 relaxation times provide information on ion dynamics, showing slower dynamics at higher salt content, and in the Na-ion system compared to the Li-ion.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ionic liquids (ILs), typically consisting of organic cations and organic or inorganic anions, have attracted significant attention as safer electrolyte alternatives for metal-ion batteries [1]. Unlike conventional organic electrolytes, ILs exhibit lower flammability, non-volatility, high thermal stability, enhanced electrochemical stability, and high solubility of organic and inorganic compounds. Given that ILs are composed of cations and anions, various ion combinations can produce numerous possible ILs with unique properties [2,3], and metal salts have been incorporated to produce IL-based electrolytes for energy device applications. Particularly, IL electrolytes consisting of ether-functionalized cations and perfluorinated anions like bis(fluorosulfonyl)imide, [FSI], and bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide, [TFSI], have demonstrated potential in enhancing Li or Na-ion dynamics, a crucial factor governing electrolyte performance in metal-ion batteries. The flexible alkyl group in R-O-R alicyclic cations and high electron density of the oxygen is known to promote lower viscosity and greater electrochemical stability of the IL [3–8].

To date, the investigated cationic ether groups encompass various configurations, including piperidinium-based and pyrrolidinium-based cations with single and multiple oxygens along the cation chain [9,10]. Recently, Sourjah et al. highlighted the oxazolidinium (C2moxa) cation for its higher ionic conductivity ILs over non-ether pyrrolidinium cations, while Warrington et al. investigated methoxy-functionalized ammonium-based and morpholinium-based cations, the latter featuring a single oxygen within a ring structure [11–13]. The studies by Shimizu et al. and Nam et al. indicate that ether oxygens in cations weaken Li anion interactions and reduce solvation numbers, with density functional theory (DFT) calculations showing that ether-functionalized groups in ILs can act as electron donors, influencing charge distribution and cation-anion interactions [10,14]. Ether-functionalized IL electrolytes are also being studied for sodium-ion batteries (NIBs) due to the potential global lithium supply shortage, with similar benefits in terms of reducing the electrostatic interactions between the Na ion and IL anions [9].

The solvation and speciation of the target Li/Na ion is very important as it determines (1) how the metal cation will be transported through the bulk electrolyte and (2) desolvation and related processes at the electrode surface such as intercalation, plating/stripping and solid electrolyte interphase formation [15]. Apart from physical and electrochemical characterizations, spectroscopic techniques are commonly used to understand the influence of electrolyte composition and chemistry on the solvation and transport of Li/Na ions. Particularly, NMR spectroscopy offers significant advantages in studying dynamics and interactions in ILs, especially when used in combination with calculations [16–22]. For example, the study of Martin and colleagues utilized 1H 7Li Heteronuclear Overhauser Effect Spectroscopy NMR to observe the Li-ion interaction with the oxygen of an IL cation's ether group, showing close agreement with molecular dynamics simulations [22]. Moreover, Zhu and colleagues used 1H chemical shifts (δ) to identify the impact of varying sample temperatures and peak widths to understand the molecular motions of the diethylmethylammonium cation in a protic IL [23].

The 17O isotope has scarcely been utilized to study these materials, especially in systems with high concentrations of metal salts where increased sample viscosity makes 17O NMR particularly challenging. Various other nuclei for NMR are often present in IL electrolytes, such as 1H, 19F, 13C, 7Li, 23Na, and 31P, and have all been exploited in NMR applications, but as oxygen sites play a direct role in metal-ion coordination, the direct investigation of oxygen in these IL electrolytes has much potential to provide new insights [18,24]. For this reason, conducting 17O NMR is expected to be very useful in unravelling new information on the influence of oxygen-containing electrolytes with metal ions, but such studies of IL electrolytes without isotopic enrichment and without solvent additives are rare [18,24,25]. This is understandable given the unfavorable properties of the 17O isotope. Owing to the quadrupolar nature of this nucleus (nuclear spin number of 5/2), 17O NMR signals are sensitive to both perturbations in the local magnetic field and to the local electric field gradient (EFG). As fluctuation of the EFG generally leads to short T 1 and T 2 relaxation times, its NMR peaks tend to show broad widths, reducing resolution and sensitivity [23,25]. Compounding this issue, the isotope has an extremely low natural abundance of 0.038 %, as well as a relatively low resonance frequency (67.8 MHz at 11.7 T), resulting in an inherently weak signal strength. Nonetheless, the use of neat samples (i.e., without solvent dilution) and short recycle delays can potentially provide high signal-to-noise (S/N) spectral ratio and acceptable NMR sensitivity in a reasonable experiment time [18,24]. Its T 1 relaxation times can also be measured fairly quickly and can be analyzed to provide information on nanosecond (ns)-timescale dynamics [18].

In the existing literature, most 17O NMR studies in materials science have been applied to oxygen-enriched systems, ceramics, organic crystals, oxygen-bound heavy transition metals, and electrolytes [25]. The 17O NMR work of Peng and co-workers focused on a series of neat glymes of various ether chain lengths with Li trifluoromethanesulfonate ([Tf]) and LiTFSI salts [26]. They compared oxygen δs in metal salts with glymes and neat glymes, finding that the former are more shielded. They also studied the line widths (LW) of glyme samples with increasing ether chain lengths, showing that LW increases and is related to the reduced molecular mobility as viscosity increases. Moreover, Bogle et al. reported the change in 17O δ in ethylene carbonate, dimethyl carbonate, and their mixtures at various concentrations with constant Li salt content [27]. Interestingly, the δ of the various ether 17O in the series of glymes reveal Li-ion preference to interact with CH2-O- CH2 sites than the terminal -O-CH3. More specific in the anions, Peng et al. compared 17O in [Tf] and [TFSI] anions in the glyme salts [26]. They found out that [TFSI] exhibits higher δ than [Tf] owing to the increased electron pair donating capacity of [TFSI]’s oxygen. In the works of Zhao et al., 17O NMR has been reported in 1,1,1-trifluoro-2,3-dimethoxypropane (TFDMP) which is a novel glyme electrolyte [28]. Based on their 17O δ, the strongest [FSI] anion and Li-ion interaction is observed in LiFSI with TFDMP, followed by the sample with 1,2-dimethoxypropane, and then with dimethyl ether, a result consistent with the Raman and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy results. In terms of 17O T 1 relaxation time measurement, Yasaka and Kimura used this technique with 17O enriched carbon monoxide to study its rotational dynamics with ILs containing imidazolium, phosphonium, and ammonium cations paired with [TFSI] anions, as well as inorganic molten salt and various alkanes [29,30]. From the viewpoint of rotational dynamics, they determined whether the IL environment is polar or nonpolar in comparison with conventional liquid solvents. In a subsequent study by the same group, they probed the rotational dynamics of 17O enriched carbon dioxide in ILs, mainly with [TFSI] anion but varying cations (1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium, 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium, tributylmethylphosphonium, and tetraoctylphosphonium) [30].

To date, the study of Newhouse & Damodaran is the only known report conducting 17O NMR on pure ILs containing no organic solvents or glymes [24]. They reported 17O spectra for the acetate anion in neat N,N-trimethyl-N-butyl ammonium acetate and N-methyl-N,N,N-tributyl ammonium acetate ILs. Although their 17O NMR spectra were of high quality, the authors did not comment on how long they took to acquire (e.g. number of scans). To the best of our knowledge, no 17O NMR studies of IL electrolytes with Li or Na metal salts incorporated have yet been reported, and this is most likely due to their increased viscosity which significantly broadens 17O NMR peaks making them even more difficult to observe. There is therefore significant potential for 17O NMR to provide new insights into IL electrolyte systems, most especially since oxygen-containing IL electrolytes are being increasingly investigated. Herein, we report for the first time a detailed 17O NMR study of select ILs and IL electrolytes featuring both increased concentrations of Li and Na metal cations as well as ether-functionalized cations. We explore the feasibility of these measurements and analyze the experimental parameters (δ, LW, T 1 relaxation times), using both molecular dynamics (MD) and DFT calculations to aid interpretation where possible. The impact of sample temperature and the incorporation of Li+ and Na+ metal cations at different concentrations are explored. We demonstrate that 17O NMR can be a useful and very feasible probe of IL electrolyte interactions and dynamics that can be carried out alongside measurements of other more commonly studied nuclei.

The salts LiFSI (99.9 %), LiTFSI (99 + %), NaFSI (99.9 %), and the ionic liquids 1-methyl-1-(2-methoxyethyl)pyrrolidinium FSI ([C2O1mpyr][FSI]) (99.9 %), [C2O1mpyr][TFSI] (99.9 %), N-(3-methoxypropyl)-N-methylpyrrolidinium FSI ([C3O1mpyr][FSI]) (99.9 %), [C3O1mpyr][TFSI] (99.9 %), and N-propyl-N-methylpyrrolidinium FSI ([C3mpyr][FSI]) were purchased from Solvionic and used as received. The tetraethylene glycol dimethyl ether (TEGDME – G4) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used as received. The 1-methoxymethyl-1,1,1-trimethylammonium TFSI ([N111,1O1][TFSI]) and N-ethyl-N-methyl-oxazolidinium FSI ([C2moxa][FSI]) were synthesized according to literature procedures [11,12]. The organic structures of the ether functionalized cations and perfluorinated anions are shown in Fig. 1 . Different molar ratios of the metal salt in the ILs were prepared as specified in Table 1 . To prevent water contamination, the storage and preparation of the samples were performed inside an argon glovebox with O2 and H2O contents less than 0.1 ppm. The samples were packed in 5 mm glass tubes without any solvent. For the 17O NMR experiments with an internal reference, the IL samples were packed in 3 mm capillary tubes inside the glove box. Then, the tubes were flame-sealed outside the glovebox and were inserted into 5 mm NMR tubes with deuterated dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO‑d 6) (99.9 %, purchased from Novachem) as outer layer for a δ reference in addition to enabling field lock. For the purpose of this study, only the [C2O1mpyr][FSI] systems, which include the neat IL, with 20 mol% and 50 mol% LiFSI and NaFSI, were subjected to 17O NMR experiments with an internal reference and 17O T 1 relaxation time measurements.

The sample purities were verified using liquid-state NMR where the nuclei 1H, 19F, 13C, 7Li, and 23Na were probed. All NMR measurements were conducted at a magnetic field strength of 11.7 T on a Bruker Avance III 500 MHz wide-bore spectrometer and a 5 mm probe. Each measurement was preceded by a temperature equilibration period of 5 min to ensure consistent thermal distribution throughout the sample.

Due to the quadrupolar nature of 17O, its NMR peaks can be significantly broader than those of spin-half nuclei such as 1H or 19F, so the standard single-pulse excitation sequence (Fig. S2a) was not employed due to potential loss of signal during the pre-acquisition delay that is required between the end of the pulse and the beginning of the signal acquisition. Consequently, the Hahn echo pulse sequence (Fig. S2b) was utilized to reverse the effect of signal dephasing and to recover the magnetization in the form of a spin echo by applying a 180o refocusing pulse. This allows the acquisition of the full signal decay, though it should be noted that the measured relative peak intensities may be impacted due to potential differences in their T 2 relaxation times. For the purposes of this study, this information is not useful as these relative concentrations are already known. Thus, the information provided by the 17O spectra comes in the form of the peak position or δ, which gives information on the surrounding electronic environment, and the LW, which is related to the transverse relaxation time and is sensitive to dynamics on that timescale (typically μs to ms). 17O T 1 relaxation times were also measured and are discussed.

The 17O NMR spectra were acquired using a Hahn echo pulse sequence with a 90° pulse length of 26 μs, an echo delay of 10 μs, and a recycle delay of 0.1 s. The sample temperatures were regulated within a temperature range of 253–373 K in steps of 10 K using a Bruker BCU, with a precision of ±0.1 K. A 5 min equilibration time was employed to achieve thermal homogeneity before each measurement. Chemical shifts and LWs were obtained by peak fitting using the DMfit software, with uncertainty values determined by manual adjustments to the fits [31]. The number of scans per spectrum was 125,000 with an experimental duration of 4 h 17 min and the spectra are referenced to 0 ppm using the 17O NMR signal from water, except for the 17O NMR experiments with the internal DMSO‑d 6 reference, which were acquired with 450,000 (15 h 26 min) to 1,800,000 scans (almost 62 h) and were referenced to the DMSO 17O signal at 13.5 ppm. Where the DMSO‑d 6 was used for the internal reference, field locking and auto shimming were also conducted.

Spin-lattice/longitudinal relaxation times (T 1) for 17O and 19F nuclei of the IL samples were measured from 253 to 373 K in steps of 10 K. For the 17O nuclei, a saturation recovery echo pulse sequence was employed with 16 values of delay times ranging from 0.001 μs to 100 μs (Table S1). Measurements were carried out separately for each 17O NMR peak to enable better saturation. The number of scans were varied from 2400 to 3200 for the anion peak with a recycle delay of 10 μs (approximately 2 h per measurement). For the cation peak, 9600 scans were acquired, taking approximately 7 h per measurement. The 19F T 1 measurements were achieved using the inversion recovery sequence with 21 relaxation delays from 1 μs – 2 s, while the recycle delays were set to >5 T 1 for the latter experiments. The number of scans per delay was 2 which results in 4 min per T 1 measurement. T 1 values were obtained from the resulting data using TopSpin. Subsequently, equations derived from the Bloembergen-Purcell-Pound (BPP) theory (Eq. n. 1) were used to fit the temperature dependence of the 17O T 1 and 19F T 1 data where minima were visible [32]. In the BPP expression, the constant K depends on the dominant interaction, while ω
o is the Larmor frequency, and τ
C is the rotational correlational time. For the 17O nuclei, the expression for the quadrupolar interaction (Eq. n. 2) was used where ν Q is the quadrupolar frequency (Eq. n. 3). In eq. 3, S is the spin number of oxygen (5/2) and C
Q is the quadrupolar coupling constant (Eq. n. 4), where eQ is the quadrupole moment, V
33 is the highest principal component of the EFG tensor, and h is the Planck constant (6.626 × 10−34 J s). To distinguish the CQ values from NMR and DFT, they are labelled CQ NMR and CQ DFT, respectively. While for the 19F nuclei, the expression for the dipolar coupling interaction (Eq. n. 5) was applied where μ
0 is the permeability of free space (equal to 4π × 10−7 N A−2), γ is the nuclear gyromagnetic ratio, ħ is equal to h (Planck's constant)/2π, and r is the distance between the two coupled spins. The C
Q
NMR and chemical shift anisotropy were assumed to be the dominant interactions contributing to the relaxation for the 17O and 19F nuclei, respectively. For the 17O T 1 data of the anions' oxygen in neat [C2O1mpyr][FSI] and with 50 mol% NaFSI, and the cations' oxygens of the entire IL system, no T 1 minima were visible so these were fitted using an Arrhenius expression (Eq. n. 6). In this expression, the temperature (T) is in Kelvin (K), R is the ideal gas constant (R = 8.314 J K−1 mol−1), activation energy (EA) therefore has units of Joules per mole (J mol−1), and the pre-exponential constant (τ 0) is the correlation time at infinite temperature. The uncertainty values were determined by adjusting the fitting parameters manually and evaluating the goodness of the fit.

The all-atom MD simulations of ionic liquids (ILs) were carried out using GROMACS 2022.3 [33]. The non-polarizable CL&P force field was adopted for FSI, while the force field for [C2O1mpyr] was adopted from a previous study [21,34]. The non-polarizable force field uses reduced charges to partially account for polarization, weakening ion–ion interactions and generally increasing ion mobility and diffusion compared with NMR results. The experimental diffusion coefficient values and its methodology are shown in Table S2 and Section S1, respectively. On the other hand, the polarizable force field accounts for electronic polarization and can improve accurate diffusion coefficients (Table S3); however, its substantial computational cost renders it impractical for the extended simulations necessary for the study of the interactions/structure of our ILs. To account for polarizability effects and improve system dynamics, the charges of the cation and anion were scaled down to 0.7 for the IL with LiFSI and 0.8 for the IL with NaFSI. The generated force fields were validated against NMR diffusivity data as shown in Table S3, focusing on reproducing relative diffusivities across species or systems rather than exact NMR values.

A simulation box containing 256 ion pairs was prepared using PACKMOL [35]. The initial system underwent energy minimization using the steepest descent (STEEP) algorithm. Following minimization, the system was annealed by raising the temperature to 700 K and then cooling it to 393 K using an NPT ensemble. The annealing process lasted 18 ns and employed Berendsen pressure coupling to ensure proper mixing of cations and anions, facilitating rapid system optimization. After annealing, the system was equilibrated using an NPT ensemble for 20 ns at 363 K, adopting Nose-Hoover temperature coupling and Parrinello-Rahman pressure coupling to achieve system stability. Subsequently, an NVT ensemble was conducted for 50 ns to calculate ion diffusion. All analyses were performed using VMD and TRAVIS [36,37]. The spatial distribution function (SDF) analysis was performed to examine the distribution of ions around specific reference atoms, providing insights into the local structural organization of the system. This analysis was conducted using the TRAVIS software, which enables detailed characterization of atomic spatial arrangements in molecular simulations [35]. The rotational dynamics of the oxygens of [FSI] anion and fluorine were studied by calculating rotational autocorrelation functions (c(τ) of a period τ) of selected vectors of SO, SF and S1-S2 in FSI at 363 K, using the eq. 7. The results are presented in Fig. S1. Using the expression in eq. 8, the τC of the Li and Na-ion systems were calculated.

The DFT calculations were conducted using Gaussian, extracting the oxygen isotropic nuclear magnetic shielding (17O σiso) and EFG tensors. Firstly, the molecular structures of [C2O1mpyr] cation and [FSI] anion were constructed, and geometry optimization were conducted at B3LYP/6311 + G(d,p) level of theory and the frequency was checked. The Li ion and Na ion were added with the cation and anion based on the coordination geometries shown in Table S4. The 17O σiso values were obtained from the output files of NMR = (GIAO,spinspin,mixed), while the EFG tensors were from the Prop = EFG. The 17O σiso values and EFG tensors for the four oxygens of the anion were averaged, representing the bulk average observed by the 17O NMR. The EFG tensors were used to calculate the CQ DFT using eq. 4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 17O NMR spectra of [C2O1mpyr][FSI] and other ether-functionalized IL electrolytes
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2, the 17O δ of the sulfonyl oxygen of the [FSI] sits between 163 and 169 ppm, while that of the methoxy group of the [C2O1mpyr] cation is located between −24 to −33 ppm. These δs are also observable in both 20 mol% and 50 mol% LiFSI. The anion oxygens have a more positive δ than the cation indicating that the 17O nuclei on the former are more deshielded than the latter. For the oxygen of the [C2O1mpyr] cation, shielding occurs due to the less electronegative methyl and alkyl groups bonded to the oxygen (−R-O-CH3), resulting in increased electron density around the oxygen nucleus. This outcome is comparable to the results of Peng et al. wherein their 17O peaks of perfluorinated anions are positioned at higher δs than the methoxy group of dimethyl ether (DME) [26]. Regarding the 17O LW, the [C2O1mpyr] cation has a significantly broader peak than the [FSI] anion. This may be attributed to the bulky pyrrolidinium ring of [C2O1mpyr] cation restricting the rotational motion of its ether oxygen group, resulting in its shorter 17O T 2 relaxation time than the oxygen of the [FSI] anion, though a difference in the size of the quadrupolar coupling interaction may also play a role. According to the CQ DFT values obtained from DFT calculations (carried out on static, isolated ions), the [C2O1mpyr] cation CQ DFT falls around 12 MHz while that of the [FSI] anion is around 8 MHz.

Both the local structure (i.e., the electric field gradient at the 17O nucleus which determines the value of CQ) and the rotational dynamics of the ion (quantified by the rotational correlation time τc), can impact the 17O T 2 relaxation time and, in turn, the NMR peak width. The effects of changes in molecular dynamics on the μs to ms timescale are manifested in Fig. 2 in two ways. Increasing salt concentrations and decreasing temperature (from 373 K to 283 K) both cause broadening of 17O LWs due to increasing sample viscosity, leading to reduced T 2 relaxation times. In terms of the temperature, the peak from the [C2O1mpyr] cation is broadened beyond detection below around 303 K in all three samples, while the signal from the [FSI] anion in the 50 mol% LiFSI sample is also lost below the level of the noise at 293 K. Conversely, high sample temperatures (> 343 K) provide acceptable S/N of the 17O NMR at all salt concentrations due to the narrower LWs. These differences in LWs enable significantly shorter experimental durations at higher temperatures owing to the increased sensitivity. Some results obtained at lower temperatures are excluded from the discussion below because their noisy spectra led to imprecise peak positions. It should be noted that higher magnetic field strengths are expected to further reduce the peak widths and decrease experiment times due to the reduced 17O second order quadrupolar interaction, which should make the acquisition of room temperature spectra more feasible. For example, Marekha et al. (2015) demonstrated that by measuring the oxygen of the carboxylate group in aqueous 1-n-butyl-3-methylimidazolium acetate at room temperature, the NMR resolution and sensitivity are improved at higher field strength (21 T) [38].

As illustrated in Fig. 3, the feasibility of acquiring 17O NMR spectra from other ether-functionalized systems was demonstrated in ILs including [FSI] or [TFSI] paired with non-functionalized [C3mpyr], and ether-functionalized, [C2O1mpyr] and [C3O1mpyr] pyrrolidinium cations, as well as the ammonium-based [N111,1O1]+, and [C2moxa]+. For the latter system, 50 mol% LiFSI salt was incorporated as [C2moxa][FSI] is a solid at 363 K [11]. Additionally, we examined one ethereal solvent, TEGDME-G4. The 17O NMR spectra of different functionalized ionic liquids and a solvent can be conducted using their neat samples without additional solvents (except for [C2moxa][FSI]) or internal reference at elevated temperatures. The experimental duration and number of scans depend on the sample and the required signal to noise ratio. For instance, the [C2moxa][FSI] with 50 mol% LiFSI required to be conducted at increased number of scans (1,000,000 scans, which took approximately 34 h). Herein, the oxygen peaks coming from the borosilicate glass NMR tube can also be observed as broadened peak beside the cation oxygen of [C2moxa][FSI] and must be considered during spectral peak fitting. On the whole, both anions and cations for the ILs show peaks within distinct δ ranges, similar to those of [C2O1mpyr][FSI]. Their peak assignments are presented in Fig. 3 where the blue broken box represents the oxygen of the anion (blue labelled atom) and the red broken box is for the oxygen of the cation (red labelled atom). For TEGDME-G4, the δs of its two distinct oxygens having unique chemical environments sit around 0 ppm and −30 ppm which corresponds to the oxygen located in the middle of the glyme chain (blue) and the terminal oxygen (red), respectively. Overall, high temperature 17O NMR can provide good spectra with high S/N ratio from a range of IL electrolyte systems.

The change in 17O δs (Δδs) upon addition of Li/Na salt and as salt concentrations increase reflects the change in the oxygen's local electronic environment [26,27]. It is important to note that the 17O NMR δs represent the average environment of a particular molecule's oxygen moiety, encompassing both uncoordinated oxygens and oxygens coordinated with Li or Na ions. Focusing on [C2O1mpyr][FSI], Fig. 4a presents the 17O δs of the anion differences relative to the neat IL (∆δ [FSI] = δ (IL with metal salt) – δ (neat IL)) for the [C2O1mpyr][FSI] IL with 20 mol% and 50 mol% of LiFSI or NaFSI at varying temperatures. As mentioned above, some results at lower temperatures are excluded because of the unreliable peak positions brought by their noisy spectra. Generally, all ∆δ [FSI] values for each sample are the same within the uncertainty level across the measured temperature range, indicating that the oxygens of the [FSI] anion maintain the same average local environment even as temperature increases. The uncertainty values are generally larger for the 50 mol% salt systems due to broader LWs. Since the cation 17O δ differences ∆δ [C2O1mpyr] were small as well as more difficult to measure accurately due to the much broader peak widths, separate measurements with an internal reference (DMSO‑d 6) as shown in Fig. S4 were performed and their ∆δ results are shown in Fig. 4b at only a single temperature (363 K).

The impact of adding the metal salts to the neat IL is clearly observed from the positions and magnitudes of ∆δ[C2O1mpyr] and ∆δ[FSI]. As the y-axis at zero corresponds to the 17O shifts for the neat [C2O1mpyr][FSI], the negative ∆δ values indicate shielding of the IL's oxygen caused by the presence of Li/Na ions. Conversely, positive ∆δs reflect deshielding. In terms of the [FSI] anion, the ∆δ[FSI] in the ILs with Li/Na salts possess negative values at all temperatures. This indicates that Li/Na anion coordination results in a net shielding of the four oxygens of [FSI] anion [27]. As for the effect of increasing salt concentration, the 50 mol% Li/Na salt concentration (1:2 metal cation to anion ratio) has a larger magnitude of ∆δ[FSI] than the 20 mol%, reflecting the greater number of anions interacting with the metal cation. Comparing Li and Na-ion systems, the more negative ∆δ[FSI] for the 50 mol% Li compared to Na shows that the Li cations have a greater net impact on the shielding of the anion oxygens, perhaps due to their smaller size.

Comparing the effects on the cation oxygen shifts to those of the anion, the magnitude of ∆δ[FSI] is clearly much greater than the ∆δ[C2O1mpyr] (Fig. 4b), implying that the metal cation interacts more strongly with the oxygen of the anion than that of the cation, as one would expect given the respective charges of these ions. The ∆δ[C2O1mpyr] values for both metal ions are essentially zero within the uncertainty values, except for the IL with 50 mol% LiFSI where a very small deshielding is observed, indicative of some minor participation of the cation's ether oxygen in the Li-ion solvation. Regarding the oxygen of the cation, its ether oxygen is expected to weaken the Li anion coordination as demonstrated by Nam et al. and Martin et al., wherein the electronegative charge of the ether oxygen facilitates an attraction to the Li+ ion [14,22]. To further interpret these 17O δ results we have carried out MD simulations and DFT calculations, which are discussed in section 3.3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Computational investigation of Li-O[FSI] and Na-O[FSI] coordination and oxygen magnetic shielding tensors
ce:section.ce:para: MD simulations were carried out to provide an understanding of the average local environments of oxygen sites in both cations and anions, specifically, their coordination with Li and Na ions, including the Li/Na-O coordination distances and coordination numbers (CN). The effects of salt concentration and temperature on Li/Na-O coordination are also discussed, and how such coordination with metal ions affects the 17O σiso were further studied using DFT.

The Li-O[FSI] and Na-O[FSI] radial distribution functions (RDFs) were first calculated for Li/Na-FSI coordination, as shown in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5a and b compare Li-O[FSI] and Na-O[FSI] coordination at both 20 and 50 mol% salt concentrations. A shorter Li-O[FSI] coordination distance (2.21 Å), read at the first RDF peak, is found compared to that of Na-O[FSI] (2.55 Å), due to the higher charge density of Li+ and its stronger electrostatic interaction with FSI. The CN of Na-O[FSI] (6.2), on the other hand, is higher than that of Li-O[FSI] (5.2), likely due to the larger ionic radius of Na+ (1.02 Å) than Li+ (0.73 Å) and its more diffuse electron cloud. These CNs remain unchanged across two salt concentrations, indicating that the proximity and number of nearest oxygens of [FSI] anion coordinating with Li and Na ions remain consistent regardless of salt concentration.

MD simulations with polarizable force fields were also carried out to compare with the non-polarizable results, with methodologies detailed in Section S2. As shown in Fig. S5 for Li-O[FSI] and Na-O[FSI] RDFs, although the positions of the first RDF peak and the CNs are slightly lower when using the polarizable force field, the overall conclusions remain consistent. Furthermore, the effect of temperature on Li-O[FSI] and Na-O[FSI] coordination was also investigated in Fig. S5. A slight decrease in CN is captured with increasing temperature, likely due to enhanced thermal motion of Li+ and Na+. This may slightly reduce the deshielding effect at higher temperatures, in agreement with the experimental results for 17O ∆δs of [FSI] anion in Fig. 4a.

Although the Li-O[FSI] and Na-O[FSI] CNs remain the same at two salt concentrations, the CN of FSI anions around Li+/Na+ increases at higher salt concentrations, as shown in Fig. 5c-5d. The first Li/Na-N[FSI] RDF peak, located between 2 and 6 Å, consists of three subpeaks corresponding to three coordination geometries in the first solvation shell: coordination with N (A1), bidentate coordination with both O atoms on two sides of N and monodentate coordination with O atoms on one side of N. The average CN increases from 4 to 4.4 for Li+ and from 4.4 to 5.2 for Na+ when salt concentration increases to 50 mol%. These CNs are also supported by polarizable force field simulations in Fig. S6a, where the percentage of different coordination shell sizes are calculated. The predominant [FSI] coordination number is 4 for 80–90 % Li+ and Na+. The CN of 5 and 6 increases at 50 mol%, particularly at a higher percentage for Na+.

Furthermore, the CNs of Li+/Na+ surrounding [FSI] were also calculated and found to increase from 0.8/0.9 at 20 mol% to 2.2/2.4 at 50 mol%. This is known to be due to the formation of (Li/Na)x-FSIy ion aggregates at high salt-concentrations [39], resulting in increased monodentate Li+/Na+-FSI coordination, as reflected by the growing A3 peak in Fig. 5d. Such changes are also evident in the SDF plots of metals ions around the [FSI] anion, as shown in Fig. 6 and Fig. S6b. In the SDF analysis (TRAVIS), the FSI anion was fixed at the center, and the 3D spatial distribution of Li/Na was computed from the full simulation trajectory, with the resulting isosurfaces representing the time-averaged Li/Na coordination sites around FSI. As FSI contains two oxygen atoms on each side, monodentate binding was defined as Li/Na density localized near the oxygen atoms on only one side, whereas bidentate binding was defined as Li/Na density simultaneously located near both oxygen atoms of the same FSI, typically surrounding its middle region. In the figure, the green contour map depicts the spatial distribution of metal ions, with areas A and B corresponding to bidentate (Li coordinated to two oxygens on the same anion) and monodentate (Li coordinated to one oxygen on the anion) coordination modes, respectively. The B regions clearly increase at 50 mol%, indicating an increase in metal ions via monodentate coordination. Understanding this shift in [FSI] coordination environment is important because different coordination modes affect the 17O σiso differently, which are discussed in the following DFT section.

The metal-ion coordination with the oxygen of [C2O1mpyr] was investigated by RDFs of Li-O[C2O1mpyr] and Na-O[C2O1mpyr] in Fig. 5e and f. Small peaks appear at a distance of around 2 Å, reflecting the direct coordination of the metal cation with the ether oxygen of the IL cation. The LiO coordination distance is 2.26 Å, closer than that of NaO at 2.50 Å. The peak intensity is higher in the Na case, which is related to the higher NaO CN (0.08) compared to the Li-O[C2O1mpyr] CN (0.02). Nevertheless, their CNs remain low, reflecting the fact that Li/Na-[C2O1mpyr] coordination is rare, which supports the less significant change in experimental NMR δ for the oxygen of [C2O1mpyr] cations with 20 mol% of salt in Fig. 4b. At 50 mol% both Li and Na CNs increase slightly, however, a small ∆δ [C2O1mpyr] was only observed experimentally in the Na system. Therefore, DFT calculations were carried out to further understand the 17O NMR shifts due to the change of coordination environment of the cation and anion. As mentioned above, the 17O shift values measured experimentally reflect the average environment of the oxygens of the anion, including those not interacting with the Li/Na ions, while the DFT results focus on the effect only for those with Li/Na coordination. Table S5 presents the 17O σiso and their average value for different coordinated structures. Taking the 17O σiso in the individual cation or anion in gas phase as a reference system, when a Li+ coordinates with a single [FSI] or two [FSI] anions through bidentate coordination (structures 1 and 2), the ion pairs form which have been normally observed in the low salt concentration ionic liquids. Their 17O σiso increases slightly, indicating a net shielding effect on O from Li coordination. The same is observed for the Na+ coordination but with a relatively higher change in shielding. These observations agree well with the small changes in shielding for 20 mol% systems in Fig. 4a.

When the salt concentration increases to 50 mol%, the coordination of [FSI] with more than one Li/Na increases due to formation of Li/NaxFSIy aggregates. Therefore, the 17O σiso for Li2FSI and Na2FSI complexes (structures 3, Table S5) were also calculated. According to the SDF of Fig. 6 and Fig. S6b, one metal ion is placed above the N to form a bidentate coordination, and another metal ion was attached to one side to form a monodentate structure. Two stable conformers were obtained for Li2FSI (3a and 3b), but only one was obtained for Na2FSI (3b) through gas phase optimization. The presence of multi-metal ions increases the overall shielding effect, which is consistent with the NMR results for the 50 mol% systems (Fig. 4a). In addition, while for the two equivalent structures, Li2FSI_3b and Na2FSI_3b, the Li2FSI_3b has a slightly smaller change in shielding tensor, opposite to the experimental trend, the Li2FSI_3a in fact has a larger tensor change than Na2FSI_3b. The Li2FSI_3a structure was supported by SDF results in both Fig. 6 and Fig. S6b, while the Na2FSI_3b structure was identified in polarizable MD simulations in Fig. S6b. Nevertheless, the variation between different conformers supports the possibility of greater 17O shielding effects for the LiFSI salt.

Finally, the 17O σiso was also calculated for [C2O1mpyr] cation with and without coordination with Li+ and Na+. Interestingly, Na coordination leads to a shielding effect whereas Li coordination leads to a deshielding effect, which is also consistent with NMR results in Fig. 4b.

Fig. 7a and b show the effect of temperature on the 17O NMR LW for the [C2O1mpyr][FSI] system, which are related to the local dynamics of (and around) the oxygen moieties in the cation and anion. Both the [C2O1mpyr] and [FSI] oxygen signals are observed to have decreasing LW as temperature increases, reflective of the increasing rotational dynamics of the cations and anions which result in increased 17O T 2 relaxation times as seen in 17O NMR studies of other liquid systems [26,40]. The cations show higher 17O LW than the anions across all temperatures and Li/Na salt concentrations, reflecting the slower rotational dynamics of the cations as already discussed earlier. Moreover, the 50 mol% samples have increased LW[FSI] and LW[C2O1mpyr] than the 20 mol%, reflecting the rise in sample viscosity associated with an increased level of ion-ion interactions at elevated salt concentrations, which is a well-known effect [41].

In terms of the effect of the different metal cations, the data suggests that the rotational dynamics of the anion are more significantly affected by the addition of Li/Na than those of the cation, with a larger change in LW upon salt addition. This is consistent with the stronger interaction with the Li/Na compared to the IL cation. Upon examining the 17O anion and cation LWs for the 50 mol% Li/Na salt systems, it is noteworthy that the Na system has slightly higher LW than the Li system, particularly at lower temperatures. The same general case applies to the 20 mol% salt concentration's 17O LWs of the anion. This is consistent with the work of Forsyth et al., who found that Na salt ILs (especially [C3mpyr][FSI]) exhibit higher viscosities than their Li salt counterparts [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: 17O T 1 relaxation times of [FSI] anion in [C2O1mpyr][FSI]
ce:section.ce:para: The temperature-dependent 17O T 1 relaxation times of the anion were measured at various Li/Na salt concentrations in [C2O1mpyr][FSI] and are shown in Figs. 8 along with fits made using BPP theory derived expressions where curvature in the data due to T 1 minima are present, or an Arrhenius expression otherwise. The parameters extracted from these fits are shown in Table 2 . The BPP fitting provides activations energies, (temperature dependent) correlation times and quadrupolar coupling constants, as well as the positions and values of the T 1 minima. These parameters relate to the rotational dynamics of the oxygen moieties on the ns timescale. For the anion oxygens, the relaxation should mainly result from the rotations of the SO2F groups around the SN bond, as well as rotations of the anion as a whole. Changes in the anion solvation (e.g., with neighboring cations) may also contribute. For the neat IL and IL with 50 mol% NaFSI, no observed T 1 minima or curvature in the data was observed (Fig. 8), with the T 1 minimum occurring outside the measurable range (limited at lower temperatures due to peak broadening and at higher temperatures by hardware restrictions). For the remaining samples, the data points are limited in the region of the T 1 minimum and the peak broadening at lower temperatures also increases the experimental uncertainty in the 17O T 1 data for ILs with salts. These issues impose limitations on the accuracy of the parameters in Table 2, however, some initial observations can be made.

As the concentration of LiFSI salt increases, both Ea and τc increase, and the T 1 minimum shifts to a higher temperature, all indicating slower dynamics. This is consistent with the 17O LW[FSI] in Fig. 7a. Specifically, the τc indicates the timescale for the dynamics of the [FSI] anion, showing the average time for the interaction tensor to rotate by one radian. Both parameters increase with the ratio of metal cations, which are solvated by the oxygens of the [FSI] anion and hinder the rotation of the anion (consistent with increased sample viscosity) [43]. Comparing the metal salts, the 20 mol% NaFSI shows higher Ea and τc values than 20 mol% LiFSI, correlating with its broader LW and the generally higher IL electrolyte viscosities for Na compared with Li [42]. Regarding the 17O quadrupole interaction, the CQ NMR increases with higher Li salt concentration, indicating anion coordination to Li ion. This again aligns with the 17O LW[FSI] data in Fig. 7a, where the increased relaxation broadening correlates with the larger size of the quadrupolar interaction. Furthermore, the CQ NMR values and the magnitude of T 1 minima are similar for 20 mol% LiFSI and 20 mol% NaFSI, indicating a similar oxygen environment for the anions. This is corroborated by the CQ DFT values from the DFT calculations, wherein both systems have CQ DFT of 8.8 MHz (the smaller values in Table 2 likely reflect dynamic averaging not accounted for in the DFT models).

ce:section.ce:section-title: 19F T 1 relaxation times of [FSI] anion in [C2O1mpyr][FSI]
ce:section.ce:para: As the anion 17O T 1 relaxation times are expected to depend primarily on the same rotational motions that will affect the relaxation of the 19F nuclei (i.e., SO2F group rotations as well as rotations of the anion as a whole), we will compare them with the results of 19F T 1 analysis. The 19F NMR shows much stronger signals due to the higher gyromagnetic ratio and 100 % isotropic abundance of this nucleus, as well as narrower peaks due to its spin-1/2 (dipolar) nature, making these experiments much easier and able to be carried out over a wider temperature range.

The 19F T 1 values and fits are displayed in Fig. 9 . Generally, the 19F T 1 minima occur within the observed temperature range, allowing a more reliable fit than for the 17O data. The 19F T 1 Ea, τc, and dipolar coupling constants (d), are shown in Table 3 . Like the 17O T 1 parameter, higher metal salt concentrations clearly lead to an increase in 19F τc values, as well as the temperatures of their T 1 minima. Interestingly, the 19F Ea values of the neat IL appear to slightly decrease when 20 mol% Li/Na salts are added, while increasing greatly with 50 mol% NaFSI. On the other hand, comparing the metal salts, the Na-ion systems have higher Ea and τc than the Li-ion systems. This specifically demonstrates a higher energy barrier for the rotational dynamics of the Na-[FSI] coordination, primarily due to the Na-ion system's higher viscosity [42,43]. For the d values, the Li-ion system is the same as the Na-ion system for both 20 mol% and 50 mol% salt concentrations. This aligns with the trends observed in the CQ NMR values of 20 mol% Li/Na-ion systems in 17O T 1. Interestingly, the magnitude of T 1 minima is slightly lower in 50 mol% LiFSI than in 50 mol% NaFSI. This trend is not seen in the 20 mol% Li/Na-ion system. Lastly, the d values increase with the increase of salt concentrations, reflecting different anion arrangement due to coordination with Li/Na ion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Correlation of 17O and 19F T 1 relaxation measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Assuming that the same rotational dynamics of the [FSI] anions dominate the relaxation of the 19F and 17O nuclei, the 17O and 19F T 1 data should in principle be able to be fitted with common values for the Ea and τc. Restricting the Ea and τc values from the 19F T 1 analysis (per sample), which should be more accurate given the wider temperature range of the measurements, the plots of 17O T 1 versus the temperatures were refitted as shown in Fig. 10 . Overall, the fits are close but have noticeably different slopes, reflecting differences in activation energies. As observed in Tables 2 and 3, the Ea in 17O[FSI] is higher than the 19F across all Li/Na salt concentrations, which indicates a higher energy barrier for the oxygen dynamics of the anion. This discrepancy may be attributed to the Li/Na cations interacting more closely with the oxygen sites of [FSI] than with the fluorine, which would make the 17O relaxation more sensitive to the dynamics of the cation (IL or metal) than that of the 19F nuclei. In the case of the neat IL, the oxygen of the anion will primarily interact with the IL cation. This may explain the slight decrease in 19F Ea value with 20 mol% Li/Na salts, unlike in the 17O, which shows a significant increase in 17O Ea. Our findings from the 17O T 1 analysis of the [FSI] anion are in agreement with the study of Massaro et al. in the nonfunctionalized IL, [C3mpyr][FSI], particularly with NaFSI [16]. Their RDF results highlighted that oxygen of [FSI] anion coordinates with Na ion as part of its first solvation shell, while the fluorine of [FSI] and nitrogen are displaced further out. The fluorine is suggested to be more in coordination with other [FSI] anions rather than the Na ion.

The τc obtained from the 17O and 19F T 1 NMR analysis are compared in Fig. 11a. In general, the oxygen has considerably higher τc values than the fluorine in all metal salt concentrations. Increased τc values are also observed for 50 mol% than that of 20 mol%. Again, these differences are attributed to the influence of the additional relaxation mechanism brought by the interaction between the Li/Na ions and the anion oxygens.

The τc values obtained from the MD simulations verified these findings as shown in Fig. 11b with the τc values for the sulfur‑fluorine bond and sulfur‑oxygen bonds in [FSI] anion. Despite the difference between the theoretical and experimental τc values, they exhibit similar trends for 20 mol% versus 50 mol% metal salt concentration and for Li-ion system versus Na-ion system. The differences in calculated τc values apparent in the 50 mol% systems in Fig. 11b are attributed to statistics, as the number of sulfur-to‑oxygen bonds is twice that of sulfur‑fluorine bonds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 17O T 1 relaxation times of [C2O1mpyr] cation in [C2O1mpyr][FSI]
ce:section.ce:para: Regarding the oxygen of the [C2O1mpyr] cation, 17O T 1 relaxation measurements were possible, but demanded significantly longer experiment times due to the broader peaks and correspondingly lower S/N (Fig. 12 ). Their T 1 minima unfortunately occur outside of the measured temperature range, with peaks broadened beyond detection below 313 K for the 20 mol% Li/Na salt concentration and below 303 K for the neat IL. For the 50 mol% Li/Na systems, the 17O peak of the cation is quite broad even at relatively high temperatures. Their accuracy is lower, as shown by higher uncertainty values compared to the anions' oxygen, and their R-squared values drop when salt is added to the IL. However, the difference between the neat IL and the IL with metal salts is distinct. Examining their T 1 plot reveals that the IL with 20 mol% Li/Na salt shows shorter relaxation time than the neat IL. This also corresponds to their Ea value in Table 4 , representing the energy needed for the rotational motion of the cation, which is linked to the sample's viscosity [43]. With regards to the neat IL, comparing Ea of the neat [C2O1mpyr] cation and the [FSI] anion shows that the former has lower Ea than the latter. The LW data in Figs. 7b support this, suggesting that the rotational motion of the ether oxygen of the cation is much more restricted since its attached to the bulky pyrrolidinium ring, unlike in the [FSI] anion. In the 20 mol% samples, the Ea values for the cation dynamics are indistinguishable within the measurement error.

Direct observation of the oxygen sites in neat ether-functionalized IL electrolytes with high concentrations of LiFSI and NaFSI, and an ethereal solvent, was achieved using liquid-state 17O NMR. Good quality spectra were acquired at a moderate magnetic field strength (11.7 T) and in reasonable experimental times (several hours) using a Hahn echo pulse sequence, a short recycle delay (0.1 s) and elevated sample temperatures (higher than 333 K), enabling the oxygen's chemical environment and local dynamics to be probed via their 17O δs and LWs. The oxygen signals from the IL's ether-functionalized cations and perfluorinated anions, and from the glyme are clearly distinguished by their δs. Higher temperatures also shorten experimental times by reducing the number of scans needed. Increasing the magnetic field strength further is anticipated to narrow peak widths and speed up experiments, potentially facilitating the acquisition of room-temperature 17O NMR spectra.

The 17O ∆δ and ∆LW values reveal the impact of Li/Na salt addition. Focusing primarily on [C2O1mpyr][FSI], the ∆δs of the [FSI] anions remain consistent across a range of temperatures, indicating that the [FSI] oxygens maintain a stable average local environment despite increasing temperature. Supported by the modelling results, temperature changes have little influence on the oxygen environment of the anion, especially regarding their intermolecular distance from Li/Na. The 17O ∆δ also shows that Li/Na-anion coordination results in a net shielding of the four oxygens of [FSI], which agrees with DFT calculations. Still based on their 17O ∆δ, as salt concentration increases, the oxygen of the anion experiences increased shielding, likely due to a higher number of anions interacting with the metal cation. Additionally, the Li-ion system exhibits a stronger net shielding effect on the anion oxygens compared to the Na-ion system. Their metal-ion-to‑oxygen intermolecular distances from MD simulation shows that the Li-O[FSI] has a shorter distance than the Na-O[FSI]. Additionally, the differences among the various conformers suggest that LiFSI salt has the potential to provide a greater shielding effect in the oxygen of the anion than NaFSI.

Regarding the 17O ∆δ of the [C2O1mpyr] cations, the values are significantly smaller than those of the anion's oxygen, as expected. Most Li/Na-ion systems show a ∆δ value of zero (within measurement error), indicating that 17O NMR is insensitive to changes in their chemical environment. However, the IL with 50 mol% LiFSI exhibits a slightly deshielded cation oxygen in its 17O NMR spectra, suggesting a lower likelihood of the cation's ether oxygen participating in Li-ion solvation than with the Na ion at higher salt concentration. Backed up by the modelling results, the Na-coordination leads to a shielding effect (lower 17O σiso) whereas Li-coordination leads to a deshielding effect. Moreover, the CN values of the cation's oxygen are very small which means that most IL cation oxygens remain uncoordinated by Li/Na ions. With regards to the 17O LWs, the local dynamics of (and around) the oxygen moieties in the cation and anion align with their sample viscosities as higher LW are observed for the 50 mol% salt concentrations than the 20 mol%, and the Na-ion system than the Li. Notably, the 17O LW in the oxygen of the anion is higher than that of the cation, especially upon salt addition, which indicates that the oxygen of the anion has a stronger interaction with the Li/Na ion compared to the oxygen of the cation.

We also probed the local dynamics of/around the oxygen moieties using 17O T 1 relaxation measurements. The oxygen relaxation in [FSI] anion and [C2O1mpyr] cation come from the SO2F groups around the SN bond and the whole anion, and the ether group of the cation, respectively. Despite the limitations brought by peak broadening at lower temperatures and the T 1 minima occurring outside the measured temperature range for some IL samples, especially for the oxygen of the cation, observations can still be made from their T 1 parameters which are the Ea, τc and CQ NMR, as well as the positions and values of the T 1 minima of some samples. For the oxygens of the [FSI] anion, particularly in the Li-ion systems, their T 1 parameters show that as salt concentration increases, dynamics become slower. Comparing the 20 mol% concentrations of the Li and Na salts, the latter has higher Ea than the former correlating with its broader 17O LW. Interestingly, their 17O quadrupole interaction and the magnitude of their T 1 minima are identical, suggesting comparable oxygen environments for the anions, consistent with the CQ DFT values. The oxygens of the anion dynamics were also compared with the fluorine of the anion using their 19F T 1 relaxation times to check if the same rotational dynamics of the [FSI] anions dominate the relaxation of the 19F and 17O nuclei. The trends observed between their salt concentrations and type of metal salts follow the same trend. However, the Ea and τc according to the 17O data are higher than in the 19F T 1 in all salt concentrations which we attribute to the oxygen of the [FSI] anion interacting directly with the Li/Na ions and thereby affecting the 17O relaxation.

In summary, we have shown that 17O NMR measurements can provide valuable information on IL electrolyte structure, interactions and dynamics, and should be considered as an additional useful experiment that can complement the more commonly studied nuclei. Directly probing the oxygen environment can provide insights into the role it plays in solvating metal ions such as Li or Na, a key factor in determining the performance of the electrolyte in battery applications.

Julius Kim Tiongson: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Sreehari Batni Ravindranath: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation. Maria Forysth: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Fangfang Chen: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation. Luke A. O'Dell: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.
